region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for canned sardine in oil; SNOMED CT coverage for processed canned fish is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of canned sardine in oil is approximately 85g (one small can drained), providing around 134 kcal, 20.6g protein, and 5.8g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Canned sardine in oil — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for canned sardine in oil (cr_id F03040021). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 175139 (Sardine, Atlantic, canned in oil, drained solids with bone) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this substitution is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Canned sardine in oil contains only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel per validator requirement (numeric field); gi_category is set to not_applicable to convey the true clinical meaning. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0 (0.1g carbohydrate × 85g serving / 100 = 0.085g available carbohydrate, GL negligible).

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 5200mg per 100g
hypertension.guidance: Canned sardine in oil contains 5200mg sodium per 100g — nearly 9 times the 600mg/100g threshold — making it unsuitable for hypertensive patients unless consumed in very small quantities (5–10g) as a flavouring; rinsing under cold water before use and choosing low-sodium or water-packed alternatives is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.1g/100g) and high protein with omega-3 fatty acids
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned sardine in oil is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content (0.1g/100g), high protein (24.2g/100g), and omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which improve insulin sensitivity and reduce metabolic inflammation; however, the extreme sodium level (5200mg/100g) warrants portion control and thorough rinsing.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids offset by extreme sodium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sardines are a rich source of cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that reduce triglycerides and inflammation, but the extreme sodium content of 5200mg/100g in this canned-in-oil preparation poses a significant cardiovascular risk; patients with CVD should use only rinsed, drained sardines in very small quantities and prefer low-sodium or fresh alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 content is anti-inflammatory but extreme sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sardines are among the richest dietary sources of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, which directly suppress TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA; however, the 5200mg/100g sodium content far exceeds safe thresholds for RA patients and thorough rinsing with strict portion control is essential.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned sardine in oil is a processed fish product consisting of whole or filleted Atlantic or Pacific sardines (Sardina pilchardus or Sardinops spp.) packed in vegetable oil and heat-sterilised in a sealed tin. The canning process preserves the fish while softening the bones, making them edible and a significant source of calcium (150mg/100g). The flesh is firm, slightly oily, and has a rich, savoury, mildly briny flavour characteristic of cured and preserved fish. Canned sardines are a globally consumed pantry staple widely used in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a protein-rich ingredient in sandwiches, rice dishes, pasta, and toast. Per 100g, this product provides 158 kcal, 24.2g protein, 6.8g fat (including approximately 1.8g saturated fat and significant omega-3 EPA and DHA), and only 0.1g carbohydrate, making it highly suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. The sodium content of 5200mg/100g is extremely high, reflecting the salt-curing and brine used in commercial canning; this figure may represent undrained product and drained values are significantly lower. Clinically, sardines are valued for their omega-3 fatty acid content, which reduces cardiovascular risk and inflammatory markers, but the sodium level makes this product unsuitable for hypertensive patients without careful preparation including rinsing and strict portion control.
common_user_queries: Is canned sardine in oil healthy?
How much protein is in canned sardines?
Is canned sardine high in sodium?
Is canned sardine good for heart health?
Can I eat canned sardines if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040021) with confidence 0.95. Fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated) estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 175139 for Atlantic sardine canned in oil, drained solids with bone, which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Sodium value of 5200mg/100g is flagged as potentially reflecting undrained product; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. SODIUM CAVEAT: The sodium value of 5200mg/100g reported by HPB may reflect undrained product including brine. Drained sardine values from USDA FoodData Central (entry 175139) report approximately 505mg/100g sodium. Users and clinicians should verify with specific brand data; drained values are significantly lower. 2. FATTY ACID ESTIMATION: saturated_fat_g (1.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.2g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 175139 (Sardine, Atlantic, canned in oil, drained solids with bone) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as sardines do not contain industrial trans fats (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific product; detailed micronutrient and moisture data are available in USDA FoodData Central for reference but were not substituted to preserve HPB source integrity for the primary record. 4. GLYCEMIC INDEX: Canned sardine in oil contains negligible carbohydrates (0.1g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel required by validator) and gi_category to not_applicable to convey the correct clinical meaning. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 5. DESCRIPTION CORRECTION: The input description incorrectly described this food as 'raw cured meat from the hind leg portion of pork' — this is a copy-paste error from a ham/prosciutto record. The correct description for canned sardine in oil has been applied. 6. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are the best available defensible matches; SNOMED CT does not have a specific code for canned sardine in oil.

All other criteria were already passing. Input had a critical description error (pork ham text on a sardine record) which was corrected in iteration 1. All mandatory multilingual names provided. Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary) with fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central 175139 (documented). Sodium value flagged with caveat.
